data.title: Stock index futures fall as Nvidia's China licensing requirement fuels trade war concerns
data.description:
Stock index futures were lower on Wednesday, as worries about a U.S.-China trade war rose on news that Nvidia will need a special license to ship graphic processing units to China.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -0.6%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -1.2%, and Dow futures (INDU) +0.1%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 2 basis points to 4.31%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 4 basis points to 3.82%.

Wall Street on Tuesday ended slightly lower in a choppy trading session, as a boost from positive bank earnings petered out amid more caution over developments surrounding tariffs.

"After a strong start to the week, the market mood turned more negative again yesterday, as tensions between the US and China showed signs of further escalation," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"So after a brief period of greater stability in markets, that reminded investors about the ongoing risks of escalation, raising fears that the trade war could still get worse from here," Allen added.

Nvidia (NVDA) fell 5.4% in premarket trade after it received a notice from the U.S. government that it needs a special license to ship its H20 graphic processing units or related hardware to China, and such a license will be required indefinitely.

"Some politicians may believe trade is more important than it is. Trade does matter economically, but a 'made in China' product in a store does not mean all the price paid goes to China," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

President Donald Trump has also directed an investigation to determine if U.S. critical minerals imports "threaten to impair national security," which could lead to tariffs or restrictions on the sector.

On the economic side, traders will keep an eye on the March retail sales report and the March industrial production data. The former is expected to rise 1.3% M/M, while the latter is expected to come in at -0.2% M/M.

March retail sales may show consumers frontrunning tariffs, Donovan added.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is also expected to speak later in the day.

data.title: Commerce Bancshares GAAP EPS of $0.98 beats by $0.05, revenue of $428.05M beats by $8.35M
data.description:
• Commerce Bancshares press release (NASDAQ:CBSH): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.98 beats by $0.05.
  • Revenue of $428.05M (+7.6% Y/Y) beats by $8.35M.
  • verage loan balances totaled $17.2 billion, an increase of 1.0% compared to the prior quarter.
  • Total average available for sale debt securities increased $66.1 million over the prior quarter to $9.2 billion, at fair value.
  • Total average deposits decreased $83.7 million, or .3%, compared to the prior quarter. The average rate paid on interest bearing deposits declined 15 basis points to 1.72%, compared to the prior quarter.
  • The ratio of annualized net loan charge-offs to average loans was .25% in the current and prior quarters.
  • The allowance for credit losses on loans increased $4.3 million during the first quarter of 2025 to $167.0 million, and the ratio of the allowance for credit losses on loans to total loans was .96%, at March 31, 2025, compared to .95% at December 31, 2024.
  • Total assets at March 31, 2025 were $32.4 billion, an increase of $368.3 million, or 1.2%, over the prior quarter.
  • For the quarter, the return on average assets was 1.69%, the return on average equity was 15.82%, and the efficiency ratio was 55.6%.

data.title: Telus announces $1.6 billion debt offering
data.description:
• Telus (NYSE:TU) has priced $1.6 billion of fixed-to-fixed rate junior subordinated notes in two series, each with a 30.25-year maturity.
  • The notes are offered through a syndicate of agents led by RBC Dominion Securities Inc., Scotia Capital Inc. and TD Securities Inc.
  • Closing of the offering is expected to occur on or about April 21, 2025.
  • The 6.25% fixed-to-fixed rate junior subordinated notes, Series CAR, were priced at $99.965 per $100 principal amount for an initial effective yield of 6.25% per annum until July 21, 2030, and will mature on July 21, 2055.
  • The 6.75% fixed-to-fixed rate junior subordinated notes, Series CAS, were priced at $99.959 per $100 principal amount for an initial effective yield of 6.75% per annum until July 21, 2035, and will mature on July 21, 2055.

data.title: China's Xpeng reviews supply chain resilience amid tariff turmoil
China facing max U.S. tariff rate of 245%?
data.description:
China now faces tariffs of up to 245% on imports of goods into the United States, "as a result of its retaliatory actions", according to part of a White House statement.

The number was included as part of a fact sheet released on April 15 in connection with an administration probe of critical minerals. 

"China now faces up to a 245% tariff on imports to the United States as a result of its retaliatory actions," a bullet point said. There was no breakdown of the figure, which is significantly higher than the recently reported figure of 145%.

President Donald Trump signed an executive order launching an investigation  into the national security risks posed by U.S. reliance on imported processed critical minerals and their derivative products.

The White House emphasized that the move is a part of Trump’s ongoing “America First Trade Policy.” 

Last week Trump issued a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries that had reached out for negotiations. But China was an exception, seeing a higher rate.

Beijing, meanwhile, has appointed a new top international trade negotiator amid tariff tensions with the U.S.

China ETFs: (KWEB), (PGJ), (FXI), (ASHR), (YIN), (YANG), (GXC), (FXP), (KBA), (CXSE), (CNYA), (CHAU), (FLCH), (YXI), (FCA), (KALL), (JCHI)

ETFs to track the S&P (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Euro Area inflation falls to 2.2% in March, meeting estimates
ASML falls after bookings miss estimates, CEO warns of uncertainty around US tariffs
data.description:
Shares of ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) fell about 4% premarket on Wednesday after the company warned that tariffs were increasing uncertainty for the long term, but maintained its annual outlook.

Tariffs and Outlook: 

"There's this new uncertainty around tariffs. And like many experts, many businesses are explaining this is, of course, something that we don't know how to quantify yet. But this is adding definitely uncertainty on the long term," said CEO Christophe Fouquet in a video uploaded on ASML's website.

However, Fouquet said that they see a lot of strength in AI. "In fact some of the demand for this year, of course, but also for next year has solidified. So that's very encouraging,"  Fouquet noted.

Fouquet noted that, adding to this, the discussion the company had with its customers, points to 2025 and 2026 to be both growth years.

ASML's CFO Roger Dassen broke down four categories by which tariffs could impact the company. These were shipments to the U.S.; tariffs on imported parts and tools; material imports required for its manufacturing in the U.S.; and tariffs from other countries on U.S. exports.

"We're very actively working with the entire ecosystem to try and minimize the overall impact on the whole ecosystem as a result of that," said Dassen.

Dassen noted that it was too early to discuss what extent of impact tariffs would have on global gross domestic product, or GDP, and total market demand, "and therefore it's quite impossible actually to put a number on that."

Revenue Outlook:

ASML kept its 2025 total net sales outlook unchanged, and expects it to between €30B and €35B (with mid-point at €32.5B).

ASML expects second quarter 2025 total net sales to be between €7.2B and €7.7B (with mid-point at €7.45B).

CEO Fouquet said in the earnings release, "artificial intelligence continues to be the primary growth driver in our industry. It has created a shift in the market dynamics that benefits some customers more than others, contributing to both upside potential and downside risks as reflected in our 2025 revenue range."

Key Metrics:

The Dutch company — which makes extreme ultraviolet lithography, or EUV, machines — saw first quarter of 2025 total net sales grow about 46.3% year-over-year to €7.74B, which missed estimates. Compared to the fourth quarter of 2024, net sales fell 16.4%.

Bookings:

The company reported bookings of €3.94B billion, which was less than the average estimate of €4.82B by analysts, according to a report by Bloomberg News. Net bookings for the EUV machines in the first quarter were €1.2B.

While reporting its fourth quarter results in January, ASML had said that it would stop reporting order bookings after 2025, "PO bookings can be lumpy and are not necessarily a good reflection or an accurate reflection of the business momentum," Dassen had said "What we will do, is provide a backlog. So, on an annual basis we will provide you with the total backlog."

China Sales:

China accounted for 27% of the total net system sales of about €5.742B, behind the South Korea with 40% share in the first quarter of 2025. U.S. and Taiwan had 16% share, each in the first quarter of 2025, according to the company's investor presentation.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, China accounted for 27% of the total net system sales of about €7.12B, behind the U.S. with 28% share. Fouquet had said then that "2025 will be a year where we see China going back to a more normal ratio in our business."

The U.S. has placed several export restrictions on companies, including ASML, to curb China's access to advanced semiconductor equipment, which, among other things, is used in making AI products. 

Dividend: ASML intends to declare a total dividend of €6.40 per ordinary share for 2024. Recognizing the three interim dividends of €1.52 per ordinary share paid in 2024 and 2025, this leads to a final dividend proposal of €1.84 per ordinary share.

Stock Buyback: ASML said that in the first quarter, it bought around 4M shares for about €2.7B, under the current 2022-2025 share buyback program.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: ASML, UAL, and more
data.description:
Stock futures slipped Wednesday morning as the Trump administration ramped up scrutiny on imports of critical minerals, following ongoing probes into pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, heightening trade tension concerns. Investors now look ahead to key retail sales data due later today for fresh clues on consumer strength.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) +6% – Shares rose after the carrier posted better-than-expected Q1 earnings of $0.91 per share, swinging from a loss of $0.15 a year ago and topping estimates by $0.17. Growth in its basic economy segment helped drive the beat. Capacity rose 4.9% Y/Y, while TRASM increased 0.5% and CASM dropped 3.4%. Excluding fuel, CASM rose 0.3%. International revenue climbed 5.2%, offsetting a 3.9% drop in domestic revenue. However, management warned that a recession could significantly dent profitability. For Q2, United forecasts EPS of $3.25 to $4.25, bracketing the $3.97 consensus. FY2025 guidance ranges from $11.50–$13.50 per share in a stable economy to $7.00–$9.00 in a recession, vs. the consensus of $10.36 per share.

Biggest stock losers

  • ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) -5% – Shares dropped after the Dutch semiconductor equipment maker reported mixed Q1 results and missed order expectations and flagged potential demand headwinds from new U.S. trade restrictions. Net bookings totaled €3.94B ($4.47B) in Q1 2025, falling short of analysts’ forecast of €4.89B. CEO Christophe Fouquet noted that while the long-term demand outlook remains strong, particularly with AI as a key driver, “uncertainty with some of our customers” could push revenue toward the lower end of ASML’s 2025 forecast range of €30B–€35B.
  • J.B. Hunt Transport Services (NASDAQ:JBHT) -6% – The stock fell as investors looked past upbeat Q1 results and focused on declining revenue and margins. Operating revenue dipped 1% Y/Y to $2.92B, dragged down by a 5% reduction in average truck count in Dedicated Contract Services, 15% fewer stops in Final Mile Services, and a 13% drop in load volumes in Integrated Capacity Solutions. Operating income slid 8% to $178.7M, weighed by lower DCS revenue, weaker yields in JBI, rising insurance and medical costs, and higher equipment and maintenance expenses.
  • Interactive Brokers Group (NASDAQ:IBKR) -9% – Shares slumped after the company delivered mixed Q1 results, with earnings falling short of expectations despite a 28% dividend hike and the announcement of a 4-for-1 stock split. The profit miss appeared to overshadow the shareholder-friendly moves, weighing on investor sentiment. The stock split will take effect after market close on June 17, with trading on a split-adjusted basis beginning June 18.

data.title: Heineken reports Q1 results; reaffirms FY outlook
data.description:
• Heineken press release (OTCQX:HEINY): Q1 Revenue of €7.78B (+1.9% Y/Y).
  • Net revenue (beia) organic growth up 0.9%; per hectolitre increasing 3.3%.
  • Beer volume organic decrease of 2.1%.
  • Premium beer volume organic growth of 1.8%; Heineken® volume growth of 4.6%.
  • Outlook for the full year unchanged; operating profit (beia) expected to grow organically 4% to 8%.

data.title: European markets dip as investors remain cautious with tariff turbulence, ASML falls
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.49% to 8,211. UK's inflation rate drops to 2.6%, below estimates. The British Pound rose for a seventh straight day to $1.327, heading for its longest winning streak since July.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.96% to 21,059 

France (CAC:IND) -0.82% to 7,275. 

The annal inflation rate in Austria slowed to 2.9% in March.

Producer prices in the Czech Republic decreased by 0.3% Y/Y in March.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) edged 0.88% lower to 503.57, with tech stocks taking the hardest hit. Investor sentiment globally remained cautious as Trump’s rapidly evolving trade policies kept traders alarmed.

Investors digested a fresh round of corporate earnings. ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) dropped 5% after Q1 revenue miss. Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY) revenue beats estimates but beer sales wane.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down 3 basis points to 4.30%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down 6 basis points to 2.49%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 6 basis points to 4.59%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK).
